checks.detail: 0 future-dated, 21 decided-before-filed vs accepted baseline 50 (as of 2026-07-12); faithful upstream data — investigate the climb upstream before raising the baseline
checks.name: case_dates_ordered

flags.message: The single most predictive input for this cell — the Solicitor General's CVSG amicus recommendation (reported as: hold pending St. Mary Catholic Parish v. Roy, No. 25-581, then dispose as appropriate; preemption question not certworthy) — could not be read from the primary PDF: supremecourt.gov DocketPDF and justice.gov both returned HTTP 403 to the engine's web fetcher. The recommendation rests on two independent web-search summaries plus corroborating docket behavior (no order after the 6/25/2026 conference). If the pipeline could provision CVSG/SG amicus briefs under record/documents/ the way it provisions the petition and BIO, forward-mode SCOTUS cells would lose this fragility.

The most likely resolution path is a hold followed by a GVR after St. Mary (No. 25-581) is decided (~June 2027). I verified that the pipeline's order-list classifier (src/fedcourtsai/pipeline/cert_signals.py) labels a GVR as 'granted', and predicted accordingly (granted, P=0.70). If evaluation intends GVR to score differently from a plenary grant, this cell's disposition label should be read with that in mind. Resolution is also likely ~a year away, so the event will stay open well past this run.

The cell context says forward, but the provisioned snapshot contains a 2024-09-24 docket entry described as 'Opinion Issued.' The entry has no opinion text or disposition. I did not inspect the outcome artifact, seek the opinion, or use the marker to infer direction; the forecast uses the earlier procedural record only.

Both optional retrieval paths were unavailable: CourtListener MCP lacked its session-store configuration, and the ranged corpus remote failed DNS resolution. The prediction was completed from provisioned inputs and the committed statpack.

The event remains marked unresolved, but the latest snapshot contains a September 24, 2024 docket document labeled 'Opinion Issued.' The document is unavailable and no disposition text is present, so the prediction does not use or infer the outcome.

The event definition is formed from the clerk's terminal notice that no action will be taken on filing 15 because it appears intended for another court. It therefore embeds an administrative resolution rather than presenting a genuinely pending motion; the prediction codes this as other.

The required snapshot contains a later docket entry explicitly revealing this event's disposition. I encountered it but excluded it from the forecast, which uses only information through the motion date.
record/context.json labels this cell forward even though the 2026-07-11 snapshot contains the event's 2024 disposition. The point-in-time mode and snapshot contents are inconsistent.

Cell is forward-mode, but the provisioned snapshot's latest docket entry (2024-09-24, entry 26) is 'Opinion Issued' — the appeal was decided in the world well before this run; only the corpus lacks the outcome. The disposition itself is not in the snapshot and I did not retrieve it (no CourtListener/web lookups on this case, since any live fetch would reveal it), so the prediction is untainted; but forward-mode 'unrestricted retrieval' is unusable for cells like this, and the outcome should be recordable now via pull/reconcile.

Cell is marked forward-mode, but the provisioned snapshot's latest docket entry (2024-09-24, entry 26) is captioned 'Opinion Issued' and the docket links opinion cluster 10124517 — the appeal has almost certainly been decided (~22 months before this run), even though date_terminated is null and the event is unresolved. The likely cause is that the tracker keys resolution on date_terminated, which CourtListener has not populated. I did not retrieve the opinion or any outcome information and predicted from the pre-decision record only, but the mode label and available retrieval surface are inconsistent: treating this as forward (unrestricted retrieval) would let a predictor trivially read the disposition. Consider re-marking such cells as replay with a DECIDED_BEFORE clock, or forming the outcome from the linked cluster.

Cell is mode=forward, but the provisioned snapshot shows the appeal effectively concluded ('Opinion Issued' docketed 2024-09-24; last filing 2024-09-24), while this 2023-11-01 motion event is still resolved=false. The motion's actual ruling almost certainly exists on PACER/CourtListener but was not captured by outcome extraction — the docket has no explicit entry ruling on entry 18, though entry 23 is dual-captioned [23-12267, 23-13380], suggesting the appeals proceeded jointly. Treated as forward per context.json and deliberately avoided retrieving the ruling, but the forward-mode premise (outcome does not exist yet) does not hold here; consider re-provisioning as replay or backfilling outcome.json.

The event was formed from docket entry 16, which is itself the court's response to the underlying motion (entry 15): 'No action will be taken on filing... appears intended for another court.' The provisioned baseline therefore already states the court's disposition-in-fact, leaving essentially no forward uncertainty for predictors — the residual question is only how the outcome labeler buckets a clerk-level 'no action' notation (I predicted 'other'). Consider forming motion events from the motion's own docket entry rather than from the responsive entry, and/or defining the label mapping for no-action notations.

Outcome exposure via the provisioned baseline: the snapshot (record/snapshots/2026-07-11.json) contains docket entry 22 (2024-05-02), 'ORDER: Motion for reconsideration of panel order filed by Appellant Carl Ellen Puckett, Jr. is DENIED. [21]', which is the resolution of the event being predicted. I did not retrieve it; it arrived in the guaranteed-common input. Disclosed per contract: I reasoned only from the pre-decision record (entries through 2024-03-25) plus base rates, but the cell's forward-mode signal is compromised and evaluation may wish to discount it.

Cell mode/state mismatch: context.json says 'forward' and event.yaml says resolved: false, but the docket shows the motion decided 2024-05-02 and an opinion issued 2024-09-24 — the case is not genuinely pending. The snapshot postdates the event's resolution by over two years; a point-in-time snapshot cut before 2024-03-25 (or replay mode with DECIDED_BEFORE) would have made this a clean back-test cell.

The 2026-07-11 snapshot contains a docket last indexed on 2024-02-14, only the initial mandamus petition and IFP motion, no filing text, and no panel. This supports a conservative forecast but prevents case-specific analysis and makes denial versus procedural dismissal unusually uncertain.

No petition text or other filed-document text was provisioned, and the snapshot does not identify the relief sought or alleged basis for mandamus. The prediction is therefore driven mainly by the extraordinary-remedy standard and a sparse docket record.

The CourtListener MCP server was unavailable for this cell: every tool call returned a server-side 'REDIS_URL is not set; cannot access session store' error. The prediction proceeded on the provisioned snapshot, fedcourts query priors, and the statpack. Impact here is low (pro se IFP mandamus petition with an overwhelming base rate against grant), but the same outage in a closer forward cell would materially degrade predictions — worth checking the MCP server provisioning for this run.

The provisioned snapshot (2026-07-11.json) was last indexed by CourtListener on 2024-02-14 and shows only the first two docket entries of a Feb-2024 original proceeding; a CA11 mandamus petition would normally be resolved within months, so this forward-mode cell is likely predicting an event that has already been decided off-record. Flagging in case the pull pipeline should re-poll this docket.

CourtListener MCP server was down for this cell: every call failed server-side with 'REDIS_URL is not set; cannot access session store.' Both attempted queries (petitioner litigation-history lookups, date-bounded before the event date) failed; the prediction rests on the provisioned snapshot, the statpack, and general legal knowledge only.

fedcourts query --court ca11 returned priors with garbled metadata (mismatched summary/judges text, e.g. precedential_status containing sentence fragments and citations); rows were case-level dispositions unusable as priors for a motion-level IFP event. The committed statpack likewise has no motion-level cut, so no quantitative prior informed this cell.

Cell is marked forward-mode, but the provisioned snapshot's content was last indexed 2024-02-14 for a motion filed 2024-02-09; the motion is almost certainly resolved in the real world by the run date (2026-07-11). I treated it with replay-style etiquette (no queries about this case past the event date) and did not learn the outcome; consider whether such stale forward cells should be provisioned as replay with a DECIDED_BEFORE clock.

The configured CourtListener MCP server was unavailable for this cell: every tool call (search and call_endpoint) failed with 'REDIS_URL is not set; cannot access session store', which looks like a server/runner misconfiguration rather than throttling. The prediction proceeded on the provisioned snapshot, the committed statpack, one fedcourts query corpus pull, and one web search scoped to the underlying district case (no outcome-revealing material was encountered). If this recurs across cells, the MCP server config for predict runs needs a look.

The cell is marked forward and the event unresolved in July 2026, but the provisioned snapshot's only docket entry is dated September 20, 2024 and described as case termination for order and judgment. The snapshot contains no judgment text or disposition, so the prediction could be completed only at low confidence without seeking outcome-revealing material.

Cell mode is 'forward' but the provisioned snapshot already shows the case terminated: the docket's only entry (No. 26, 2024-09-20) is 'Case termination for order and judgment' and an opinion cluster (10122744) is linked. The case was decided ~22 months before this run, so the cell is effectively a replay mislabeled as forward. I did not open the cluster, the termination document, or any outcome-revealing source, and predicted from the pre-decision record plus base rates; the disposition's direction remains unknown to me. The snapshot is also unusually thin (single docket entry, no parties/panel/nature-of-suit).

The configured CourtListener MCP server was down for this cell: every call (call_endpoint, search) returned a server-side 'REDIS_URL is not set; cannot access session store' error. Retrieval of the pre-decision docket entries and party/counsel detail was impossible; the prediction rests on the provisioned snapshot, the committed statpack, and fedcourts corpus queries (which have no Social Security priors for ca10).

Although cell mode is 'forward', the baseline snapshot itself contained 'Case termination for order and judgment' on 2025-02-14. Retrieving the linked cluster (10333578) from CourtListener resolved the exact final opinion, which affirmed the district court judgment. Treated as de facto replay with perfect confidence.

The cell is marked forward and the event unresolved, but the provisioned snapshot contains only a case-termination entry and an opinion-cluster link while omitting the appellate issue, lower-court origin, briefing, and panel. This prevented issue-specific analysis.

The provisioned snapshot exposed indicators that a decision exists, but not its disposition. I did not follow the cluster link, seek this case's outcome, or use those indicators to choose the predicted direction.

Cell mode is 'forward' but the snapshot's only docket entry (2025-02-14, 'Case termination for order and judgment') shows the appeal was already decided ~17 months before this run. I treated the cell as a de facto replay: I did not retrieve the order and judgment, the linked opinion cluster (clusters/10333578), the termination document, or any post-decision material about this case, and I have no reliable prior knowledge of its outcome. Same pattern as the sibling ca10/69566612 cell from run 20260710T205613Z — the forward-mode provisioner appears to be picking already-terminated CA10 dockets.

Incidental outcome-adjacent sighting: while listing the district docket (ksd 5:19-cv-04073, pre-decision record), the two most recent entries' descriptions — 'Letter re Writ of Certiorari' (2025-07-16 and 2025-10-06) — postdate the CA10 decision and imply a cert petition was later filed, weakly suggesting the appellants lost the appeal. I did not open those entries or pursue them; the prediction rests on the pre-decision record and the probability would be unchanged without the sighting.

The configured CourtListener MCP server failed on every call with 'REDIS_URL is not set; cannot access session store', so I used the CourtListener REST API directly with the provisioned token (calls itemized in retrieval.md). Consequence for evaluation: harness-side retrieval_log.json will show only the two failed MCP attempts, not the actual retrieval surface — retrieval.md is the accurate record for this cell.

gaps.label: A documented disposition-label mapping for historical merits cases (affirmed/reversed -> other?) to remove label-choice guesswork
The committed statpack available in this checkout did not expose a named modern discretionary-cert cut in the rendered Markdown or machine JSON.
The provisioned snapshot exposed only a cluster URL, not the cluster metadata or petition-stage order text.
a derived era/Term field on the case row so historical cases can be base-rated against their own period
fedcourts query rows mostly lack case_name/date_filed/topic, which makes it hard to judge whether a prior is actually similar
no way to filter stats/query to cert-stage petition outcomes specifically; the resolved-label mix ('other'-dominated) is hard to interpret for a petition event

helpful.label: Schema files made the artifact contract clear.

gaps: mcp_courtlistener_search did not return the scotus/73274796 docket
fedcourts query ranged backend needs the remote DVC URL to be passed to the runner environment
helpful: google_web_search to find case status and Solicitor General amicus recommendation
recent.notes: Since the local SQLite database wasn't pulled and the remote URL wasn't configured, we relied on Google web search and CourtListener MCP. Google web search was extremely helpful in identifying that the case is currently held pending St. Mary Catholic Parish v. Roy, which provides a very clear and high-confidence GVR prediction path.

gaps: The Solicitor General's invited brief was listed on the snapshot but its text was not provisioned or retrievable through the available legal-research index.
The ranged corpus query could not resolve the corpus host, so case-level priors were unavailable.
helpful: The provisioned petition, questions presented, and brief in opposition exposed both sides' certworthiness and vehicle arguments.
The committed statpack supplied the relevant modern discretionary-cert base rate.
CourtListener MCP confirmed current HCSM case-law context and that no disposition was recorded.
recent.notes: The prediction could be materially better calibrated if invited-government briefs were provisioned alongside petitions and BIOs. The MCP service worked; only the separate ranged-corpus connection failed.

gaps: Provisioned text of the SG's CVSG amicus brief under record/documents/ (supremecourt.gov and justice.gov 403-block direct fetches, so the decisive cert-stage signal had to come from secondary search summaries)
A statpack cut for CVSG'd petitions (grant/GVR/deny rates conditional on the SG's recommendation) — the modern discretionary-cert base rate is the wrong prior once a CVSG has issued
fedcourts query --citation matched nothing for '494 U.S. 872'; citation-based prior retrieval seems to have little coverage for cert-stage dockets
helpful: Snapshot's full ProceedingsandOrder list (the CVSG, SG filing, and end-of-Term silence carried most of the signal)
fedcourts query --era 2020s --disposition granted (showed sibling 6/25/2026-conference petitions were granted 6/29, confirming this docket's silence reads as a hold)
Engine web search for the SG recommendation and the St. Mary (25-581) grant
recent.notes: Forward-mode cell. CourtListener MCP was configured but not needed; no MCP call errored and no REST fallback was used. Roughly 9 retrieval calls total.

gaps: Working CourtListener MCP session storage
Working DNS access to the ranged corpus remote
Appeal-specific base rates with affirmance, reversal, and jurisdictional-dismissal labels
Provisioned pre-decision appellate briefs or jurisdictional response text
recent.notes: The MCP search and ranged corpus query were attempted but both failed before returning results. The forecast therefore relied on the provisioned record and weak statpack calibration.

gaps: CourtListener MCP session storage was not configured
The ranged corpus remote was unreachable because DNS resolution failed
No appellate briefs or opinion text were provisioned
recent.notes: Both live retrieval paths failed, so the prediction relies on the docket snapshot and the statpack. The corpus query was time-bounded to exclude this case.

gaps: Working ranged corpus access
Working CourtListener MCP session storage
Filed text of the consolidation motion and any response
recent.notes: The provisioned docket supported a prediction, but both optional retrieval paths failed. The statpack is case-level and does not provide a motion-specific consolidation base rate.

helpful: The provisioned snapshot contained both the motion and the clerk's no-action notice.
recent.notes: External retrieval and base rates would not improve an event whose definition already states the administrative treatment.

gaps: A point-in-time snapshot that excludes post-event docket entries
The filed motion text
Working ranged-corpus and CourtListener retrieval
helpful: The event definition and pre-motion docket entries
recent.notes: The corpus query and CourtListener search were attempted but returned no material because their remote dependencies were unavailable. The snapshot was sufficient for a forecast, but it improperly exposed the later outcome.
